turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: After the group meeting, the leader praises you in front of everyone for what you did. Lily stands next to you, staying quiet a little too long.

turns.speaker: Lily
turns.text: Uh… that part was actually something I revised since yesterday. But whatever—maybe it’s fine.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: The vibe suddenly freezes. People glance back and forth. Your chest heats up—embarrassed and annoyed at the same time.

choices.text: Say right away: “Yeah but you still did most of it anyway, so what?”
choices.playerLine: Yeah but you still did most of it anyway, so what?

choices.text: Own it in front of the group: “Oh yeah… Lily actually fixed a lot back then. I’m sorry—let me clarify it with the leader.”
choices.playerLine: Oh yeah… Lily actually fixed a lot back then. I’m sorry—let me clarify it with the leader.

turns.speaker: Lily
turns.text: When you say it like that… it sounds like I’m making a big deal. I’m just telling the truth.

turns.speaker: Lily
turns.text: …Thanks for saying that. I’m not trying to steal credit—I’m just scared people will misunderstand.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: The leader walks over and asks quietly: “Wait, what happened just now?” Lily stands there, eyes slightly red.

choices.text: Pull the leader aside and explain privately so Lily won’t feel exposed.
choices.playerLine: Let me tell you privately, so it doesn’t get awkward.

choices.text: Say it in front of the group to correct it immediately, even if it’s awkward.
choices.playerLine: Let me be clear: Lily fixed a lot of that part, and I kinda took the praise too easily earlier.

choices.text: Change the subject so Lily gets the hint to ‘not make it a big deal.’
choices.playerLine: Probably just a misunderstanding—no big deal. I’m gonna keep cleaning up, okay?

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That night, you open your phone and see Lily text: “I’m not mad. I’m just sad because it feels like you needed to win more than you needed me.”

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You type a reply. Your fingers stop a few times.

choices.text: Text a clear apology, no excuses—then ask how Lily wants you to make it right.
choices.playerLine: I’m really sorry. Earlier I was protecting my reputation. Please give me a chance to make it right—what do you want me to do?

choices.text: Send a ‘soft explanation’ to keep your image, but promise to be more careful next time.
choices.playerLine: I didn’t mean it like that. It was crowded so I reacted too fast. Next time I’ll be more careful.

outcomes.text: You choose to humble yourself from start to finish. Lily softens, and the group learns how to give fair credit without embarrassing anyone.
You tell the truth publicly—painful but clean. Lily respects you more, and you lose your “status” to gain peace.
You win the line, but you lose the closeness. Lily slowly pulls away, and you end up feeling lonely—even in the group.
You admit part of it, but you still keep an escape route. Nothing breaks, but that little thorn of “excuses” stays.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You post about the team project, accidentally putting your name first and describing your contributions a little too thoroughly. The post is blowing up.

turns.speaker: Kyle
turns.text: Hey… did you see what you posted? Some people on the team read it and it’s kinda… awkward.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You reread the post. You realize: you didn’t lie—but the way you told it makes you look like the “main character.”

choices.text: Edit the post right away, put the team first, then apologize in the group chat.
choices.playerLine: Let me edit the post. The way I told it earlier really does sound off.

choices.text: Leave it as-is and tell yourself: ‘I actually did the work—why should I be scared?’
choices.playerLine: I really did do the work… they’re just being sensitive.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: Someone on the team comments under your post: “Thanks for mentioning the team 🙂” It’s polite… and it stings.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: How are you gonna respond? Whatever you say now, people will read it super closely.

choices.text: Reply directly under the comment, admit you missed the mark, and tag everyone with their real roles.
choices.playerLine: You’re right. I told it unfairly. Let me tag everyone properly—thanks to the whole team.

choices.text: Don’t reply publicly—just DM that person an apology so you don’t look dumb.
choices.playerLine: I’ll DM them… replying publicly is so embarrassing.

choices.text: Reply with a little sarcasm: “Uh, I did mention you 🙂”
choices.playerLine: Uh, I did mention you 🙂

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That night, you see Kyle send a screenshot: someone in the team chat said, “Forget it—let’s not work together anymore.”

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You realize: what you wanted wasn’t just recognition… you wanted to be seen as the best one.

choices.text: Offer to step down as lead for a while and take a support role to rebuild trust.
choices.playerLine: I want to step down as lead for a while. I’ll take the support work, and you all decide the direction.

choices.text: Hold your stance: ‘If they get it, they get it’—focus on delivering results to prove yourself.
choices.playerLine: Whatever. I’ll just deliver results, and they’ll see.

outcomes.text: You choose to lose the “spotlight” to keep the people. The team doesn’t trust you overnight, but they can tell you’re being real.
You edit and apologize, but you avoid the public part. Things calm down, yet it still feels like you’re more scared of losing face than losing people.
One reply crashes everything. You keep being “right,” but you lose the chance to keep walking with the team.
You deliver results… but the win feels cold. You look around and realize fewer people want to celebrate with you.

scenes.title: A Comment in Front of Her Dad

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You’re having dinner at Maya’s house. Her dad asks about work. You get excited talking and accidentally call Maya’s field “easy.”

turns.speaker: Maya
turns.text: When you say it like that… it sounds like the years I worked for it were nothing.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: Maya’s dad gives an awkward smile and goes quiet. You feel hit right where you’re most confident: your knowledge.

choices.text: Brush it off with a joke: “I’m just messing around,” to cool it down fast.
choices.playerLine: I’m just messing around—Maya, you’re so tense.

choices.text: Apologize immediately in front of her dad—no dodging.
choices.playerLine: I’m sorry. That was disrespectful. I didn’t understand enough, but I still judged.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: On the way home, Maya doesn’t talk much. You remember her dad’s eyes: not angry, just disappointed.

turns.speaker: Maya
turns.text: Honestly… I’ve always been insecure about my field. You hit the exact spot that hurts most.

choices.text: Stay quiet for a moment, then ask: “How do you want me to make it right?”
choices.playerLine: I’m sorry. What do you want me to do so you can feel respected again?

choices.text: Explain for a long time—‘that’s not what I meant’—trying to prove you weren’t wrong.
choices.playerLine: I mean… I was talking about the market, that’s all. I wasn’t looking down on you.

choices.text: Stay silent because you can’t face it, and let time wash it away.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: Back home, you look in the mirror. You realize: you talked tough because you’re scared people will think you’re just average.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You consider doing something concrete to “make up for it”—not just with nice words.

choices.text: Post a public note recognizing Maya’s ability (but ask Maya first).
choices.playerLine: I want to honor you properly. I’ll ask you first, and only post it if you’re okay with it.

choices.text: Buy a gift to “close the case,” so you don’t have to talk more.
choices.playerLine: I’ll buy something for Maya… that should fix it.

outcomes.text: You humble yourself at the hardest moment. Maya sees you’re not just saying sorry—you’re truly showing respect.
You apologize, but you still want to be “right.” Maya cools down, but that thin layer of hurt remains.
You avoid it and move on. Maya doesn’t argue, but she doesn’t open up to you like before.
You use a gift to cover the awkwardness. Things might smooth out, but you know you still haven’t faced your ego.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: In your Bible study group, Talia shares about a failure. You can tell she’s sinking into self-pity.

turns.speaker: Talia
turns.text: I feel so useless. Honestly, I don’t even know if God can still use me.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You have the “right” words in your head. But you can also see Talia needs a hand—not a lecture.

choices.text: Say bluntly: “Your faith is weak. Stop complaining.”
choices.playerLine: Your faith is weak. Stop complaining—get up already.

choices.text: Sit closer: “Tell me more—I’m listening.”
choices.playerLine: Tell me more. I’m listening.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: After that meeting, you hear someone whisper: “Talia cried in the bathroom.” You flinch—you thought you were helping.

turns.speaker: Talia
turns.text: I’m not mad at you. It’s just… what you said made me feel even worse.

choices.text: Own it: “I wanted to look strong, so I spoke harshly.”
choices.playerLine: I’m sorry. I wanted to look like I was “right,” so I spoke way too harshly.

choices.text: Push back: “But I was right—you needed to hear it.”
choices.playerLine: But I was right… you needed to hear it so you could be stronger.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That night, you pray and keep getting stuck on one question: ‘Do I want to be right… or do I want to love?’

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: What will you do at the next meeting?

choices.text: Share briefly with the group: you hurt Talia, and you want everyone to learn to speak with love.
choices.playerLine: I want to apologize because my words hurt Talia. And I want you all to help remind me to speak with love.

choices.text: Keep it private and just care for Talia quietly.
choices.playerLine: I’ll care for Talia quietly. I won’t bring it up in front of the group.

outcomes.text: You humble yourself and let the whole group learn with you. Talia no longer feels alone in her pain.
You apologize sincerely and care quietly. The relationship heals, but you still dodge the discomfort of losing face in public.
You keep your version of being “right.” Talia doesn’t argue, but her trust in you gets worn down.
You spoke harshly first, then owned it. There’s still hope, but the price is swallowing your pride.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You work with Hugo. Your boss praises you in front of the whole office. Hugo gets stuck with the “trash cleanup” work in the back.

turns.speaker: Hugo
turns.text: Congrats. You’re really good… and I guess I’m just built for the small stuff.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You hear the bitterness. And you also remember: the times you got to “shine” were because Hugo carried things behind the scenes.

choices.text: Laugh it off: “Come on, we all do work.”
choices.playerLine: Come on, we all do work. Don’t overthink it.

choices.text: Tell your boss (after the meeting): the results include Hugo’s work, and ask to share credit fairly.
choices.playerLine: I want to talk to the boss about sharing credit, because Hugo carried a lot of key parts.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: The next day, your boss calls you in: “So you’re saying you’re not capable enough?” It sounds like a trap.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: How do you answer truthfully without humiliating yourself?

choices.text: Be clear: you did your part well, but Hugo’s work matters too; fairness keeps the team healthy.
choices.playerLine: I did my part well. But Hugo’s part also shaped the outcome. I want the team to last, so I’m asking for fair credit.

choices.text: Backtrack: “Never mind… I was just saying it.”
choices.playerLine: Never mind… I probably overthought it. Sorry about that.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That afternoon, Hugo texts: “I don’t need you to ‘save’ me. I just need you to see me as a teammate.”

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: How do you respond?

choices.text: Invite Hugo to plan next week together, so he’s seen and heard.
choices.playerLine: Let’s work together from the start next week. I want you in the decision-making—I don’t want you stuck behind the scenes anymore.

choices.text: Reply politely, keep the old rhythm because you’re scared of losing the spotlight.
choices.playerLine: Yeah, I get it. Thanks. If you need anything, just tell me.

outcomes.text: You choose truth with wisdom—and you choose to walk with someone. You still do well, but you don’t need to stand alone on stage.
You start well, but fear of losing your spot makes you retreat. Hugo doesn’t hate you—he just trusts you less.
You choose smooth and fast. The spotlight stays, but you feel hollow because you know someone got left behind.
You speak the right thing to your boss, but inside you still want the attention. You realize ego doesn’t die from one good action.

scenes.title: Sorry, But Still Wanting to Win

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You and Tracy argued over a misunderstanding. You already apologized, but Tracy still isn’t over it. You’re thinking, ‘I said sorry already—what else?’

turns.speaker: Tracy
turns.text: You apologized… but it sounds like you just want this to end, not like you want to understand me.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That hits your ego. You want to say, ‘Then what do you want from me?’

choices.text: Say it straight: “So what do you want me to do now?”
choices.playerLine: So what do you want me to do now? I already said sorry.

choices.text: Lower your voice: “Can you explain it to me? I want to understand for real—not just finish this.”
choices.playerLine: Can you explain it to me? I want to understand for real—not just finish this.

turns.speaker: Tracy
turns.text: I want you to admit that back then you treated my feelings like an “annoyance,” not something “important.”

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You flinch because she’s right. But admitting it means seeing yourself as the bad one—at least in your own eyes.

choices.text: Admit it: “Yeah, I minimized your feelings.”
choices.playerLine: Yeah. I minimized your feelings. I’m sorry for that—not just sorry that we “argued.”

choices.text: Argue semantics: “I didn’t minimize you—I was just busy.”
choices.playerLine: I didn’t minimize you. I was just overwhelmed, so I reacted like that.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: A week later, Tracy needs your help at the exact time you’re also stressed. This is the real test—not words anymore.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: What do you choose?

choices.text: Show up to help—even if you’re not in the mood—because you choose to love with action.
choices.playerLine: I can come. I’m tired, but I don’t want to leave you alone.

choices.text: Decline gently and keep a boundary so you don’t explode again.
choices.playerLine: I don’t think I have the capacity right now. I don’t want to help with resentment and hurt you again.

outcomes.text: You don’t just apologize—you change how you show up. Tracy can feel you valuing her more than your ego.
You own your fault and keep a wise boundary. It’s not instantly “sweet,” but it’s clean and sustainable.
You won’t admit the deepest part. Your apology sounds like a procedure—and Tracy can hear that.
You want it “resolved” more than you want to understand her. Tracy stops arguing, but her heart closes.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: You keep signing up to serve. People praise you for being “on fire.” But lately you’ve been snappy when things don’t go your way.

turns.speaker: Felix
turns.text: I’m gonna be real… lately, whenever someone gives feedback, your mood flips instantly. Are you okay?

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That hits your real fear: if you’re not praised, who are you? You’ve been doing a lot… but your heart feels dry.

choices.text: Blame it on being tired: “I’m just doing too much.”
choices.playerLine: I’m just doing too much, that’s all. It’s not like I have a problem.

choices.text: Admit it: “I’m scared of being looked down on, so I control things.”
choices.playerLine: I think… I’m scared of being looked down on. So I control things so I won’t get criticized.

turns.speaker: Felix
turns.text: If you want, you can take a week off. No one hates you. But you’re burning yourself out.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: A week off means no one sees what you’re “doing.” And that’s exactly what makes you shake.

choices.text: Accept the break, step back from the role, and pray quietly to fix your motives.
choices.playerLine: Yeah… I’ll take a week off. I want to check my motives before God.

choices.text: Don’t take a break: ‘If I stop, things fall apart’—keep carrying everything.
choices.playerLine: If I take a break, things will fall apart. I’ll just keep doing it—safer that way.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: That Sunday, when you’re not on the serving team, someone new steps up… and does a good job. You feel relieved and… slightly stung.

turns.speaker: Narrator
turns.text: How do you face that feeling?

choices.text: Congratulate the new person and genuinely lift them up.
choices.playerLine: You did great. Thanks for stepping up. I’m glad God uses many people—not just me.

choices.text: Compare quietly, then decide to come back “stronger” so you won’t be forgotten.
choices.playerLine: …I guess next week I need to come back and do it properly.

outcomes.text: You learn to serve without needing to be seen. Your ego shrinks, and real joy comes back.
You keep carrying everything to protect your position. You still get praised… until you burn out and snap at the people you claim to be “helping.”
You’re brave enough to rest, but your heart still compares. You realize: resting your body is easier than resting your ego.
You get stuck in a loop: serving to be seen, then serving more because you fear losing your spot. No one forces you—your ego does.

reflections.verseText: Pride goeth before destruction, and an haughty spirit before a fall.
questions: In this story, what am I protecting—truth or my reputation?
Am I apologizing to get peace, or apologizing to be changed?
What is one small thing I can “yield” today to practice humility?

reflections.verseText: God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble.
questions: Do I do good work to be seen, or to truly serve?
Where am I making excuses to protect my image?
What is one concrete step to give fair credit back to someone else?

reflections.verseText: Let nothing be done through strife or vainglory; but in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves.
questions: When my ego gets touched, what’s my first reflex?
Am I afraid of being looked down on, so I have to look “above”?
How can I show respect with action—not just words—this week?

reflections.verseText: Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.
questions: Do I use “being right” to win over people?
Do I want to be right more than I want to love?
Next time, how can I speak truth without breaking someone?

reflections.verseText: Be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love; in honour preferring one another;
questions: Can I share the spotlight? Why is that hard for me?
What am I afraid of losing when someone else gets lifted up?
What’s one way I can walk with a teammate “from the start” instead of from the shadows?

reflections.verseText: He that answereth a matter before he heareth it, it is folly and shame unto him.
questions: Does my apology come with real listening?
Am I playing word games so I don’t have to admit the deeper issue?
How can I practice honoring someone’s feelings in a real, practical way?

reflections.verseText: Take heed that ye do not your alms before men, to be seen of them: otherwise ye have no reward of your Father which is in heaven.
questions: When nobody sees me, do I still want to do good?
Am I serving God—or serving my sense of being “valuable”?
What’s one specific decision I can make this week to humble myself?
